sentences.text: Yes.
I met him in the first camp, in Vivikond.
And he was surprised, and I was surprised, because woman were separate and men were separated and children were separate, in separate barracks, you know.
And-- and when he saw me, he said, why did you come here?
Well, I came here because otherwise I would have been killed right then in the ghetto, because they liquidated the ghetto and all the people were killed, the rest of them.
So I said, I came because I wanted to save mine child and know that you are here.
And then I haven't-- I-- I saw him, I think, one more time.
I don't remember exactly.
But there was no food there.
Conditions were horrible.
There's no food.
There wasn't even water.
And every morning, we were taken out to work.
And I-- I remember-- I-- I worked in-- on the railway, which-- a rail fall down on mine leg, broke mine toe.
And then I worked with this potato group, which we went out of-- far away from the camp.
We walked quite far, where they had some potato [POLISH].
How you say in English.
Buried in-- because of the war, I guess, they were buried in the ground.
So we had to dig them out and find the potatoes, because there was no food to bring-- they didn't give us the potatoes.
They cooked it for the Nazis, for the soldiers, for the guards.
So every day, we went potato digging.
But sometimes I-- we used to grab a potato, the Nazi shouldn't see, and eat it, you know.
Raw potato, but something in a history.
Raw potatoes.
Pieces, what we could, you know.
But there was no food.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was all this poison water.
I don't-- so many things happened.
But the day when they-- they-- was in-- in-- in-- in Vivikond, they liquidated Vivikond seven weeks later, and they took us all to Vaivara, to the other camp.
It was winter.
Was very, very tough.
And I remember that in Vivikond and in Vaivara, they did the same thing.
They used to take-- if a man was sick-- because a lot of people were sick from the typhus and all that, the dysentery-- and couldn't go to work with the group, he used to order a [GERMAN].
Used to order his-- his helpers to take the man.
They used to throw him.
Around Vivikond, there were [GERMAN].
You know, because it was swamp.
The area was very swampy.
So they had around the camp large one, like from here to here, dug-- how do you dig it-- hole around the camp so the water would start run in this-- I don't know what-- what do you call this.
Well, in German, used to say [GERMAN].
So I don't-- anyway.
So they used to take any man, anybody who was left after the [GERMAN], after-- to-- who didn't go to work.
They used to go in the barrack and check them.
But I was at that time watching-- was-- over the children's barrack.
So they didn't bother me.
Things which come back to my mind.
But they used to find the man, if a man was staying, they found him.
They used to bring him to the-- to this big holes, you know.
Tell him to take off his clothes.
And then drop him in this swampy water.
And then they used to take the big long sticks, you know, and bang him and bang him and hit him and turn him around like a ball until he had died in the-- in the swamps.
Every single day.
And I saw it because the children's barrack was next to the swampy area there.
And I-- and-- and-- if they would know that I've watched it, they would have killed me right there.
But they didn't know-- I was the only one in the camp.
And-- and they couldn't see me.
I was hiding.
And I used to watch every day the same story.
There was always a man or two man or three man, I don't remember how much.
It would start.
They had to undress.
He had to undress and jump in to the swamp.
Was up till here, till his neck was the water.
And then they used to see the two guys, and Schnabel used to watch it.
With the big long sticks, you know.
Wooden sticks.
And bang him and hit him and hit him.
He was thrown around like a ball until he drowned there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then they used to pull him out and they used to-- they had nowhere to bury the dead people.
There was so many.
They used to burn them, like-- you know?
The woods and trees, old trees, and that-- behind the camp.
They made a big fire, and that's where they threw the bodies.
Can you take a moment and compare the conditions in the three camps that you were in in Estonia?
Were they much the same, or were they very different?
About the same.
And there was no food.
That's the fact.
The water was supposedly poisoned.
We don't know.
But we-- we took what we could.
Because it was muddy swamps, you know, in all the area.
Well, Vivikond was all swamps.
That's why they moved it, because it was sinking, you know?
There was no place where to stand.
Vaivara was better.
I-- I don't remember exactly.
I know we used to go to work with the potatoes digging, and all the other junk.
But the thing-- there was no food, and no-- no way of surviving.
And the only thing what they had was they had for the-- for the-- they used to do potatoes for the Nazis, for the guards, you see?
People were dying constantly, and all the diseases and everything.
And-- The first-- oh, excuse me.
Yeah.
And that's all right.
OK.
Right.
Then it was--
[SIGHS] Let me think, because we went-- in January, we went to Auschwitz.
So this was seven weeks from September.
October.
November.
December.
It was like-- we were like-- wasn't like-- two-- six weeks or two months, probably.
Two months.
And then we were again liquidated, Vaivara, and taken to Ereda.
That was the end, [? finito ?] [? de ?] [? fiesta. ?] That's where everything was liquidated.
Everything, in a few weeks, because-- every-- the children were very sick, and that people were dying like flies, and they were killing them, and-- and there was no survival.
There's no way of having any food.
Nothing.
Yeah.
And she got well.
Without any medication, without any food.
The only thing, she always-- she was unconscious because she had fever, very high.
So to-- where her lips were all cracked and swollen.
So she used to say water, water.
And I used to give her the water.
I didn't know.
If I would known, I would have let her die there so she wouldn't have to go through-- till the-- till-- to the chimneys.
To the gas chambers.
Right.
We went to Vaivara.
Vaivara, where she was sick.
And went to Ereda.
In Ereda, she was separated again.
And I took her-- turn around and brought her in my barrack.
And somebody told this monster that I-- I took my baby.
From Vivikond to Vaivara, and from Vaivara to Ereda.
Not long, because that's when they liquidated it.
It was, I don't know, six weeks, probably.
Each of them like two months or six-- it was from September till January.
We walked from-- from Vivikond to Vaivara, we walked.
And from Vaivara to Ereda, I think we walked too.
There was no transportation.
The only times transportation was when we went to the gas, to Auschwitz, which we didn't know nothing about.
Yeah.
I know why.
Because they didn't want to fight the front, to go on the front.
They didn't want to fight the war.
So that's why they became such big devoted Nazi bastards.
Excuse me.
That's why they keep the camps going, so they had a job, you see.
That's mine way of thinking, anyway.
Because none of them want to go to-- to-- because they were losing.
We-- I remember in January when Auschwitz was liquidated, we know we had to leave Auschwitz because the-- the-- the Russians came that same night.
They came-- they liberated Auschwitz.
Yeah.
They're the same thing.
All on the same-- but Vaivara, I think, we had a building.
Like structures, I think.
I don't remember.
But Ereda was-- was the same thing like Vivikond, this [GERMAN].
I don't know what-- how to say it in English.
I think they're called Finnish huts.
That's what the name is.
I don't know--
I think it's in English also [GERMAN].
It's somewhere in the dictionary.
In western Europe?
Elsewhere, in other parts of Europe.
We only know-- we have known one thing, that all the Jews are being liquidated, killed, period.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The only--
--one who were under-- under the Germans.
That's what German occupied and killed off all the Jews.
There was no-- the-- the men weren't-- yeah.
The last transport I remember, there were the men also.
Man and woman.
But mine-- where I stayed, I stayed with children.
It was like 90 children in one little-- in this cattle wagon.
And it was me, and it was another woman who had a child.
And there were other ones, but I don't know.
That time, I thought I could be with mine child.
That's all what I cared for.
And we had no-- no water, no bread.
I-- I-- all right.
That's why I had that blue cup hanging out.
I needed some water.
I wanted a little water.
That was the last transport from Vivikond.
And-- and they took the-- they pick-- the German, probably-- the Germans, the Nazis, they saw that-- they took the pictures of the transport.
Lanzmann.
He got it from them, and that's where-- and we had the show on television, I saw my little blue cup hanging.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because I wanted to get some water.
And I was screaming through the little window, water, water, water.
And then I hung out a little cup.
I figured maybe somebody when it stop will give me some water.
But they did not.
They didn't give a damn.
They were looking and laughing and saying this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the pictures, this-- this transport, so many cattle trains going, and this was fun for them.
10 days.
10 days coming and 10 days going.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] From the first transport, I escaped, you know, with my baby.
I ran out.
Didn't you read mine article?
It's supposed to be-- was there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] See, for me this is a very hard thing.
We came to Estonia.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they opened the cattle prisons and said anybody who has to go, you know, to the-- drink water, or-- or going to the-- because they-- when they put us in the cattle train, there was a box with bread, OK?
To last us for the 10 days.
And at the last three days, there was no bread.
But everybody, excuse me, was going in the bathroom.
The children, especially, in that box.
So the stink was unbearable, you know.
So when they opened the-- the-- the doors, people were jumping out to go into-- there was a-- a water pump.
Yeah?
I thought there's a different name.
And-- and they opened all the-- they opened all the cattle train, but my cattle train they didn't open.
Listen to that.
And I was going to the little window and saying, water, water, water.
And with me in that train was my sister-in-law with her child, mine brother's wife and little girl.
In the same train.
And finally, about-- I don't remember when it was, about 11 or 12 o'clock at night-- no, wait a moment.
I didn't-- a-- yeah.
So they opened up.
And so my sister-in-law jumped out, you know, and some-- the other people who were close to the door.
Because I was by the window with mine child.
And I-- I-- then I talked to the-- again to this guy, you know.
And I took a pot was laying there, and I said, can I also-- to one of the Nazis, I said, can-- [GERMAN].
I said, please.
We need water now, you know.
Because I was suspecting that a lot of them are not going to come back, you know?
And I wanted to have some water for mine daughter and me.
And finding a way maybe I can get my kid out and escape, you know?
So finally, one opened the door.
And he said to me, oh, there's so many people from you gone to the water.
I said, but we need water now.
Anyway, I grab the little pot and I go with him to the water, you know.
And I get some water.
And then I said to him, look-- because I had money with me, you know, when I left the ghetto.
I had like 1,500-- thousand-- I don't know how many rubles. 20,000.
Become my father-in-law before, he was taking-- he left me money, and he said to me, go with the child to our Christian friends.
They will save you.
They will hide you.
So any one I call, try to get in touch, they didn't want to do it, OK?
So the money I had with me.
And I-- when I was walking with that guard, I said, look.
I have a lot of money, and I have my gold watch, which I had on mine arm.
I will give it to you if you can let me-- me with my baby go and take out my baby.
We'll-- we'll disappear.
So he said, OK.
Fine.
So we come to that car.
I recognize-- I know which-- which-- which cattle train it was.
And he opens the iron doors from the cattle train.
And I called mine daughter, Etele, Etele, come here.
But she was a little child, and they were-- bigger people.
So they pushed her in the back and they start jumping out.
So when he saw that, he-- he whistled.
He called another couple of guards.
They came, and they threw everybody back, and me, and closed the door.
So I couldn't save myself because of the other people wouldn't let me, OK?
So listen.
But I must have been a smart girl when I was younger.
I was thinking in my head, that goddamn Nazi guard was a poor good for nothing-- they were Estonian, you know, and German and Nazis.
And I said to myself, that man is not going to rest because I told him I have money.
So he will try to get my money, OK?
That's what in my head it was.
So it got night.
And everybody starving on no-- no food and no water, nothing.
And they all lay down, you know, one on top of the other, on that little floor in the cattle train, and were sleeping.
And I was standing by the door with mine baby, you know.
I was holding my child like that by the-- by the door.
Because I had in my mind that that will happen, something.
And sure enough, about midnight, it must have been, the door opens us.
It's made the doing, you know, the iron doors.
And people start waking up.
And I grab my baby.
So the woman were-- in front of me was a woman standing, you know.
I don't remember her name.
So she jumped ahead of me.
And then I-- I threw my baby to that Nazi, to that guard.
Like that.
And I jumped.
And he closed the door.
And I put my baby on my neck, you know.
Yeah.
And the first thing he said, where's the money?
So I gave him my money, and I left with me like 1,000 rubles or something.
The rest, I gave him.
I gave everything.
And I put the baby on my neck, and I was crawling under the rails, you know, because there were like three, four rails, you know?
And this was like Siberia.
I called it Estonia Siberia.
It was nothing, you know.
Open space.
We crawled through like that.
And the woman was next to me.
And it was pitch dark, OK?
And with the last train, we were hiding right under.
All of a sudden, we-- we saw lights, lights, light.
See, he got my money, and then he called his friends and they were planning to get us back and kill us, you see.
He didn't want any witnesses.
But he couldn't find me and the baby in that moment.
They were looking all under the trains, you know, all over.
But we on the last one with the four-- four rails, you know.
They're looking, looking, looking.
They couldn't find it.
It was pitch dark, you know.
It was like 2:00, probably, in the morning.
And-- and it was late in the fall because it was open space.
So after they left, we crawled out and we start walking.
And it was start to get light.
Was probably 4, 5 o'clock in the morning.
And here were coming Nazis, generals, with-- with-- with Estonian officers, you know.
They were walking to their office.
And they looked at us.
And I-- we were wearing old clothes, you know.
Rags, you know.
And they-- they-- they wouldn't-- they couldn't believe that we were escapees, you see?
But they so-- were so-- they looked at us.
They figure, who the hell are this?
This is probably some beggars came from somewhere, you know?
Because what I did, I tore off the star.
Right away, I tore off the star.
And we came into woods, you know.
We came into woods.
And my baby was very thirsty.
We had no water.
We-- you can live without food for a few days, but you can't live without water.
And said, Mommy, water, water, water.
So we saw from far away little houses, you know?
Like farmer houses.
So we-- we stopped at the farm house, one.
And there was a-- a woman alone, an elderly woman.
And there was-- she was probably baked fresh bed for smelling, laying out, you know.
And I ask her if we can have some water and a piece of bread.
I will give her money.
So she gave us some water.
And she gave me a piece of bread for me and the child and the woman.
And I gave her-- I don't know what I had, 1,000 rubles or whatever it was.
And she said to me, you better stay away from here, because all the-- the German SS, she said, the-- all the Nazis, they're living in all those houses.
They're renting out room because it's war, you know.
So you see, watch out, she said to me.
And we went away, and we went in the woods.
We were hiding.
And we-- and then the-- the following day, same story, you know?
Mommy, Mommy, I'm thirsty.
And we were all very thirsty because was so dry there.
You-- you couldn't breathe, you know?
So we went.
Finally I saw another place, you know?
And I went in another house.
We walked, you know?
And I came into another woman, and she gave us some water and another piece of bread.
And then all of a sudden, a young man comes in the door dressed in a uniform, like a police uniform.
And he said to his mother-- was his mother.
What are they doing here?
She says, oh, nothing.
I just gave them some-- some water.
I gave her the money, you know?
All the money what I have, you know?
She didn't tell him that she got money from me.
And she-- and she-- he came in and he point a finger at me, and she says, don't you ever come by here again.
Because I'm going to take you right away, he says, to report to the SM-- to the German SS, to the gestapo, and they will kill your there.
So I walked with mine child and the woman, and we decided there's nowhere to go.
Where will be go?
What-- because that guy was standing, walking halfway with us, watching us, where we go.
He says, down there, he says, is-- is the road to-- to the concentration camps.
If you don't go there, you going to get killed.
I take you to the gestapo.
A young guy, maybe 27, 28 years old.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we walked and walked and walked.
It was getting late, like 4 or 5 o'clock.
And we were hiding in the woods.
And there was a group of people, you know, working on the road.
That's where I had mine-- on the railroad there.
And I came-- so this woman said to me-- Molly, she says, I'm not going to the camp again.
You can go with the child.
I'm not going.
So she went away.
I was left with my child.
So I came over close to that camp.
And I-- and that the Nazi, the guards shouldn't see me, you know?
I crawled down.
I said-- listen, I said, we escaped from a transport and we have nowhere to go.
How-- that was Vivikond, you know, with the-- can we go with you in the camp?
She says, how will you do it?
They are counting the people, like six people in a row, you know?
So I said, we manage.
She says, you know what?
I will give you an old coat I have to hide a baby, you know, and they wouldn't know what it is on the coat, you know.
So we waited all day long.
And in the evening, like 6 or 7 o'clock, she give me an old coat, and you know-- and I hide mine child, you know, in the coat, like I was carrying a package.
And we went in.
We were counting and counting, but they couldn't-- both the fourth or the sixth row, so they didn't pay attention to the last few.
And we went in.
And that's how it started in Vivikond.
That's what you have here.
The next day they were counting, and they didn't know who I am.
They didn't care anymore, you know?
And then they made me in charge of the children at the-- of the block.
So I-- and I saw across from them the big swampy holes where they threw the men every single day and tortured them to death.
The what?
Oh, they went to the gas chambers.
Straight to Auschwitz.
I found out six months later.
I was inquiring.
In-- in Auschwitz, I was inquiring already.
And they told me.
So mine-- mine-- my sister-in-law and my brother's child and all, they went all to the-- Auschwitz, to Birkenau, to the gas chambers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's when-- the second time they took me from death, from Estonia to Auschwitz.
That's what-- the way I got-- I got-- all the transport from Estonia went to Auschwitz to the gas chambers.
All the liquidation.
Yeah.
That was the-- six months later, that's what it was.
When they brought us in Auschwitz, it was dark, like 10 o'clock at night.
And the lights, were a lot of lights, you know.
And the-- and this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call?
Gee.
You don't know Polish, [POLISH].
You know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where you-- the-- when you frame-- when you close-- the gate.
Not the gates, the-- oh my god.
You know, when you-- where you keep prisoners, and you put a-- what do you call this, around-- the guards and the iron-- what do--
They had fences.
They had--
Fences.
Fences.
Sorry.
God, you see, what my stroke did to me?
I can't even talk anymore.
The-- the-- from big fences, like from here to here.
Very tall.
And iron, you know.
You see.
And it looked so clean and big.
And here in Eston it was filthy and dirty, the swamps, you know?
Laying on the-- on-- sleeping on the-- on the soil, on the ground, on the earth.
No water, nothing.
So this was like a paradise comparing to-- to all this camp in Estonia, you see.
So I said, gee.
Look how clean and beautiful it is, you know?
And they told us to jump out from that-- and we jumped out from the train.
And here was the man who were-- the selection, the Sonderkommando.
The one who takes all-- all the packages, you know, and the people and this.
Had very sad faces, you know?
And I didn't know why, what it is.
And I came over to someone, and I said, why are you people so sad?
It's so nice and clean here.
We were in such a terrible, filthy place.
So one said to me, did you ever hear of Auschwitz?
I said, no.
So he didn't say nothing more.
He didn't want to tell me about the gas chambers, you know.
I was so impressed, you know.
And then they took the children on-- on trucks, and the grownups, they walked.
So I said to one man, would you mind if I go with mine child?
Please.
She's a small child, you know.
So he gave me that look, because he didn't want to tell me, but the children go straight to the gas chambers, you see.
So somehow, somebody above there was watching.
I don't know who.
My father or somebody.

And he took me, let me go on the-- I was the only grownup among the children on the truck, and the-- we came to-- they drove to a building which-- a big hall where 2,000, 3,000 people can go in very easily.
I didn't know it was the back of the gas chambers, you know?
And it was so clean, and the lights, you know?
And we came in.
We're all so filthy and dirty from-- we were 10 days in filthy and in dirt, you know?
And I had in my package a-- a blouse and a pair of skirt or something.
And I said to my little girl that she should wait, and meantime go and ask the guys where is here a bathroom where you can change or wash your face, because we're so filthy.
So she-- he said, right there.
I still didn't know nothing.
Listen.
So my baby was sitting there with the rest of the kid, had the packages.
And I took mine.
And I went into that room, in that-- where there's water and a toilet.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I took off mine clothes and put on a clean blouse-- I washed my face.
I could see.
There was a little mirror there, you know.
Washed my face and put on a clean blouse and a skirt, you know, and wiped-- I had a towel with me, you know, in packages.
And I came out and I felt better.

Some-- somewhere, somebody along the line there watched over me, because five minutes later two of the men who were watching, or three of them, and watching a transport, you know, from the Sonderkommando, came over to me.
And he said to me, what are you doing in this transport?
I have to stop you.
We have to pause and--
Oh.
So you will remind me, because I forget.
